notes: All three biographies describe the same individual, Hon. Sir Andries Ferdinand Stockenstrom Maasdorp. Bios 'maasdorf_andries-ferdinand-stockenstrom_e1869' (ed. 1921) and 'maasdorp_andries-ferdinand-stockenstrom_e1871' (eds. 1908-1931) have verbatim-identical careers ('B.A., Lond., 1869; barrister-at-law... solr.-gen., Cape Colony, Aug., 1878 to May, 1897... ch. just. of the O.R.C., June, 1902; ret., Jan., 1920'), differing only by an OCR surname variant (MAASDORF/MAASDORP) and an OCR-corrupted honour year (KT. BACH. 1869 vs 1904; 1904 is consistent with the staff lists, where 'Sir' first appears in 1907). Bio 'aasdorp_a-f-s_e1878' is a one-line 1897-edition stub ('AASDORP, A. F. S.—Solicitor-general, Cape Colony, Sept., 1878.') whose surname has lost its leading M to OCR; there was a single Solicitor-General of the Cape, held by A. F. S. Maasdorp per the staff lists, so it is the same man (the Aug./Sept. 1878 discrepancy is a one-character print/OCR variance). Arguments against the duplicate grouping were considered: a same-name relative (the Maasdorp family produced several Cape jurists) could in principle account for the stub, but a relative would have had to hold the identical, unique office (Solicitor-General, Cape Colony) starting the identical year (1878), which the single continuous staff-list tenure 1879-1897 rules out. All three stints form one coherent career arc for this person (Solicitor-General Cape 1879-1897, Chief Justice ORC 1905-1909, Chief Justice OFS Provincial Division post-Union 1912-1914, retiring Jan. 1920), and would merit assignment at 'certain' confidence to the deduplicated person; they are left unassigned only because (a) the instructions forbid assigning contested stints to any member of a duplicate group, and (b) every stint is also gate-compatible with 'maasdorp_a-f-s_e1878', an out-of-case biography already linked or filtered, which is very likely yet another duplicate of the same person and may already hold these stints. Reviewer action: merge the three bio records (plus, on inspection, 'maasdorp_a-f-s_e1878') and attach all three stints to the merged identity.

The two biographies are OCR variants of the same person (MAASDORP, Christian George): identical forenames, identical opening clause 'Atty.-gen., Transvaal, Sept., 1878', resignation in Jan. (1879 vs 1880, one digit apart), and puisne judge, supreme court, Cape in Nov. (1879 vs 1885, one digit apart); the 1923 entry simply extends the career to the Union of South Africa bench. Surnames AASDORP and MAASDORF are both single-character mangles of MAASDORP, and both passed the fuzzy surname gate against the stint name 'Maasdorp, C. G'. The two stints themselves form one continuous, historically coherent judicial career (Puisne Judge, Eastern Districts Court 1888-1894; Supreme Court 1896-1909; Appellate Division/Judge President 1912-1914) consistent with the merged biography, and would be strong 'certain' links to the merged person — but a third, correctly-spelled biography 'maasdorp_christian-george_e1878' outside this case is gate-compatible with both stints and already linked elsewhere. Recommend the reviewer merge all three bio records and then attach both stints to the merged identity.

These are unambiguously the same person. The 1917-edition bio 'ACTION, ROGER DAVID' reproduces the ACTON entry word-for-word up to OCR noise: both read 'Cadet, F.M.S., Nov., 1896', land revenue collector Perak June 1900, 'asst. sec. to res., Perak, Jan., 1903', deputy registrar sup. ct. Penang May 1907, 'ag. sheriff and deputy', 'ag. 2nd mag., Singapore, Dec., 1910', 'promoted to Cls. III., Apr., 1911', '2nd mag., Singapore, Apr., 1911'. 'ACTION' appears in no staff list (7 officials surnamed Acton-family in staff lists, and the stint prints 'R. D. Acton'); the surname_gate hit was fuzzy. After deduplicating the bios into a single ACTON, ROGER DAVID record, the stint should be linked to it with high confidence: initials R. D. agree, colony Straits Settlements agrees with the Penang/Singapore postings, and at least four specific position+place+date dimensions align (deputy registrar & asst. official assignee Penang 1911; 2nd Magistrate 1912-15; Class III 1913; District Judge and First Magistrate Penang 1921-22). Recommended action: merge the bio pair, then re-run this case.

Not a genuine two-claimant contest. Bio adams_george-p_e1876 (1886 edition only) reads 'Recorder of titles, Tasmania, 3 July, 1876.'; bio adams_george-p_e1876-2 (1888-1898 editions) reads the same with an added clause 'registrar, supreme court, April, 1885.' Same person, entry updated between editions; edition ranges are disjoint and consistent with one continuing entry. Argument against duplication (father/son sharing the office) fails: both entries cite the exact same July 1876 appointment to the same post, which two people cannot both hold. Recommend merging the two bios upstream, after which the stint links cleanly: position_sim 100 ('Recorder of titles' ~ 'Recorder of Titles'), Tasmania colony match, tenure overlap 1877-1894, and edition co-occurrence [1888, 1889, 1894]. The 1877 'Solicitor-General' staff-list row is the only wrinkle (not mentioned in either bio) and would merit a glance in human review.

adams_r-p_e1867 (editions [1886], initials only) and adams_robert-patton_e1867 (editions [1888]-[1900], full name 'ADAMS, ROBERT PATTON') share the exact same appointment line including the day-precise date '20 Dec., 1867' and the same office and colony; the 1886 entry is the same person before the compiler expanded the name. The edition footprints are complementary (1886 vs 1888-1900), not conflicting, which is the signature of a single continuing entry rather than two people. No evidence suggests a second R. P. Adams in Tasmania: the staff list shows one continuous officeholder whose career (Solicitor-General then Puisne Judge from 1889) exactly tracks the Robert Patton Adams biography. Recommended action: merge the two bio records, then the stint can be assigned to the merged person with confidence 'certain'. The strongest argument against duplication - a father and son holding the same office - fails because both biographies cite the same appointment date to the day, which two people cannot share.

The two biographies are the same person printed in consecutive editions (1950, 1951) with a single OCR letter difference in the third forename (Fianyehia/Flanyehia). The 1950 entry is truncated/garbled at the end ('acctnt., 1497.' is plainly the same 'acctnt., posts and tels. dept., G.C., 1947' line, with 1947 misread as 1497). No realistic counter-hypothesis of two distinct C. K. F. Adapoes exists -- corpus context shows only 1 official with this surname in the staff lists -- but the partition rules forbid arbitrarily picking one duplicate bio, so the stint is left unassigned pending bio merge. Recommended action: merge the bios, then link the stint (would have been 'certain': initials C.K.F. exact, colony Gold Coast, position 'Accountants'/'Posts and Telegraphs' matches 'acctnt., posts and tels. dept.' verbatim apart from abbreviation, tenure 1949-1951 follows 1947 appointment).

The two in-case biographies are unmistakably one printed entry read twice by OCR: identical B.A., identical 'B. 1903', and clause-for-clause identical careers ('4th cls. teacher, Gold Coast, 1922; 2nd divn. teacher, 1923'), differing only in 'ADDO/ADDON' and 'instr.'/'inspr.' — the 1933 reading 'inspr.' (inspector) is likely the correct one given the staff list's 'Cadet Inspector of Schools'. The substantive blocker for the 1928 stint is not the duplicate pair but the outside biography addo_james-aryee_b1903 (same initials, same birth year, same colony, already linked elsewhere or filtered). Recommended reviewer action: pull the Aryee entry text; if 'Abee' and 'Aryee' are OCR variants of the same person, merge all three bio records and the 1928 stint link follows naturally (position + year agreement is strong: 'cadet inspr., schls., 1927; inspr., schls., 1928' vs staff-list Cadet Inspector of Schools 1928-1929, African Inspector of Schools 1930). The 1949 stint should stay open pending evidence bridging 1936-1949.

bullock_g_e1919 (editions 1920-1929) and bullock_g_e1919-2 (editions 1930-1937) print the identical opening clause ('Asst. traffic manager, Uganda Railway') with the same April 1919 date; the second is simply the updated entry after a March 1927 promotion, and the edition ranges are perfectly disjoint and consecutive. After merging the duplicate biographies, the Kenya 1920-1927 stint is an excellent candidate for the merged person: exact position agreement in 1920 (Assistant Traffic Manager) and again in 1927 (assistant superintendent, transportation), with the Kenya listing explained by the Uganda Railway being administered from Kenya. The Bermuda stint belongs to a different, well-documented individual (Lt.-Gen. Sir George Mackworth Bullock, K.C.B.).
